---
title: "Lab 11: ANOVA"
format: 
  html:
    toc-depth: 3
---

## Learning Objectives

## Getting started

Before you begin the lab activities, review the steps for setting up a project for a lab activity:

-   [Weekly Lab Setup](../../resources/weekly-lab-setup.qmd)

Then, complete the following steps to make sure you are fully set up.

1.  **Get the Lab Worksheet.**

    Pick up a physical copy of the lab worksheet, or print one if you are working outside of class.\
    <a href="lab-11-worksheet.pdf" download class="btn btn-outline-primary mt-3">Download Lab Worksheet (PDF, if needed)</a>

2.  **Open Posit Cloud and open the Lab 11 Assignment in the course workspace.**

3.  Create a new R script in your project and save it with a clear name such as \`lab11-anova.R\`. You will use this script to complete your work for the lab.

4.  At the top of your script, run the provided data import script so the three datasets are loaded into your environment.

    ```{r}
    #| eval: false

    source("read-data.r")
    ```

::: callout-note
## Checkpoint

At this point, you should have:

-   These instructions open in a web browser.
-   Your Lab 11 project open in Posit Cloud in another browser window.
-   Your Lab script open
-   Three datasets in the RStudio Environment tab
-   The Lab 11 worksheet in front of you.

Do not continue until all of the above steps are done.
:::

## Overview

In this lab, you will complete the ANOVA lab activity provided on the textbook website for The Analysis of Biological Data:

<https://whitlockschluter3e.zoology.ubc.ca/RLabs/R_tutorial_ANOVA.html>

The lab focuses on comparing the means of three or more groups using analysis of variance (ANOVA), a common method in biology for evaluating differences among treatments, categories, or populations.

You will use R to explore data visually, calculate summary statistics, test for overall differences among groups with one-way ANOVA, and conduct post hoc comparisons when appropriate. You will also use the Kruskal-Wallis test as a nonparametric alternative when ANOVA assumptions are not well met.

By the end of the lab, you should be able to run these analyses in R and interpret the results in biological context.

## Lab Instructions

Complete the assigned ANOVA lab activity and answer all required questions using your own R script.

Use the provided datasets and the `read-data.r` file to begin your work. Save all code used for the assignment in your script and organize it with comments so each section clearly matches the corresponding exercise.

Suggested workflow:

1.  Read each question carefully before beginning.
2.  Create the requested graphs and summary tables.
3.  Run the appropriate statistical test in R.
4.  Interpret the output in plain language.
5.  State conclusions that answer the biological question.

When asked whether assumptions are met, use your graphs and summary statistics to support your reasoning.

Write clearly and keep your script organized so another person could follow your analysis.\

## Turning In

Before leaving lab, have a Learning Assistant (LA) review your work and check off your completed worksheet.

Make sure your R script is saved and your analyses are complete before requesting a checkoff. Be prepared to explain your code, results, and conclusions if asked.

Follow any additional submission instructions provided by your instructor.
